ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic scrambled egg entry (FOODON_03301710); no specific Indian-spiced bhurji/podimas entry exists. Closest defensible parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Hen egg, scrambled — used as closest FoodEx2 anchor for spiced scrambled egg preparation
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Indian-spiced scrambled eggs. A00GJ (scrambled egg) is the closest defensible parent anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for egg bhurji / podimas preparation. SNOMED CT coverage for regional Indian egg dishes is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of egg bhurji / podimas is approximately 250 g, typically served as a main dish alongside roti, bread, or rice in a Singaporean Indian meal context.

nutrient_source.title: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Egg dishes; supplemented with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (Scrambled Eggs, cooked, NDB 01132) for micronutrient cross-reference
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN); United States Department of Agriculture (USDA)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Egg bhurji is a standard Indian egg preparation documented in ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods. Core macros (energy 150 kcal, protein 6.0 g, fat 6.67 g, carbohydrate 16.0 g) are consistent with ICMR-NIN values for egg-based mixed dishes with onion, tomato, and spice additions. The carbohydrate content (16 g/100 g) reflects the onion and tomato base rather than rice or noodles — this is a standalone egg dish. USDA FoodData Central NDB 01132 (Scrambled eggs, cooked) used for micronutrient cross-reference only. Sodium 316.67 mg/100 g reflects added salt and spice blends consistent with restaurant-style preparation. Input source (GrabFood, ai_generated, confidence 0.7) values retained where consistent with ICMR-NIN ranges.

gi_source.title: International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate for mixed egg dish with onion and tomato
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for egg bhurji / podimas in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 55 (low, per the standard GI classification scale where low = ≤55, medium = 56–69, high = ≥70) based on the following analogous reasoning: eggs alone have negligible GI (~0), but the dish contains 16 g carbohydrate/100 g from onion, tomato, and spice base. The carbohydrate fraction is predominantly from low-GI vegetables. A composite GI of ~55 is defensible by analogy with mixed egg-vegetable dishes documented in Atkinson et al. (2021) International Tables of GI Values. Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (55 × 40 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 22.0 g. Estimation basis documented per Veritas GI policy.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 316.67 mg/100 g; a 250 g serving delivers approximately 792 mg sodium, representing ~35% of the 2,300 mg daily limit recommended by the American Heart Association.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced salt during preparation, avoid additional soy sauce or condiments, and limit portion size to 150–180 g to keep sodium intake below 500 mg per eating occasion.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 16 g/100 g with dietary fibre only 1.0 g/100 g yields a low fibre-to-carbohydrate ratio; estimated GI ~55 (low boundary) and GL ~22 per standard serving indicate moderate glycaemic impact.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume egg bhurji in a 150 g portion paired with a high-fibre side (e.g., whole-grain roti or salad) to moderate postprandial glucose response, and monitor blood glucose after consumption given the moderate glycaemic load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol 126.67 mg/100 g (317 mg per 250 g serving) and sodium 316.67 mg/100 g are the primary cardiovascular concerns; saturated fat at 1.67 g/100 g is low and not a primary driver.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit egg bhurji to one small serving (≤150 g) per occasion to keep dietary cholesterol below 200 mg and sodium below 500 mg, and should request preparation with minimal added oil and salt.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory spices (turmeric/curcumin, ginger, garlic) present in material quantities; saturated fat 1.67 g/100 g is well below the 5 g threshold of concern.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Egg bhurji is generally suitable for rheumatoid arthritis patients due to its anti-inflammatory spice profile, but individuals sensitive to nightshades (tomatoes, green chillies) should monitor for symptom flares and reduce sodium by requesting less added salt.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Egg Bhurji (also called Egg Podimas in South Indian Tamil tradition) is a popular Indian scrambled egg dish prepared by stir-frying beaten eggs with finely chopped onions, tomatoes, green chillies, fresh coriander, and a blend of spices including turmeric, cumin, and garam masala in a wok or kadai over high heat. The result is a soft, moist, lightly spiced scrambled egg preparation with a golden-yellow colour from turmeric, a savoury and mildly spicy flavour profile, and a tender texture punctuated by the slight crunch of onion and the acidity of tomato. In Singapore, egg bhurji is commonly served at Indian hawker stalls and restaurants as a breakfast or lunch dish, typically accompanied by roti prata, chapati, or plain rice. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides approximately 150 kcal, 6 g protein, 6.67 g fat (saturated fat 1.67 g), and 16 g carbohydrate (fibre 1.0 g, sugar 0.67 g), with sodium at 316.67 mg and cholesterol at 126.67 mg. The dish is a good source of complete protein from eggs and contains anti-inflammatory compounds from turmeric (curcumin) and ginger-garlic. Clinically, the moderate sodium content and moderate glycaemic load (GL ~22 per 250 g serving, GI ~55) require portion awareness for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, or cardiovascular disease. The dish is naturally gluten-free, halal, and vegetarian.
common_user_queries: How many calories in egg bhurji?
Is egg bhurji good for diabetics?
How much protein in egg bhurji?
Is egg bhurji suitable for hypertension?
What spices are in egg bhurji?

confidence_reason: Core macro values from the input (ai_generated, confidence 0.7, GrabFood source) are consistent with ICMR-NIN documented ranges for Indian egg dishes with onion-tomato base. No exact ICMR-NIN entry for 'egg bhurji' as a named dish was directly retrievable; values are adapted from ICMR-NIN egg dish category and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central scrambled egg data. GI is an analogous estimate. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, B-vitamin panel, fat-soluble vitamins, and moisture data for restaurant-style Indian egg bhurji are not individually reported in ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 01132) for this specific preparation as of 2026-04-17. These fields are set to -1 per Veritas sentinel-value policy to indicate data unavailability rather than a confirmed zero. 2. The carbohydrate content of 16 g/100 g reflects the onion, tomato, and spice base of the dish — not rice or noodles. The input record's health_suitability reasoning incorrectly attributed carbohydrate to rice/noodles; this has been corrected. 3. Input ethnic_tags included 'Butter Chicken' and 'Curry' which are not applicable to egg bhurji; these have been removed. 4. Input had two portions both labelled '1 plate' with different weights (250 g and 300 g) and both marked is_default=true; 250 g has been selected as the default serving size as the more conservative estimate. 5. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate per University of Sydney GI Database policy; no direct measurement for egg bhurji exists in the approved GI database. GI 55 falls at the upper boundary of the low GI category (low = ≤55); gi_category is therefore set to 'low'. 6. Dietary tag 'vegetarian' from input retained; 'gluten_free' retained. Egg bhurji contains eggs and is therefore not vegan. 7. trans_fat_g 0.03 g/100 g retained from input — this is a trace level consistent with naturally occurring trans fats in egg yolk and is not a manufactured trans fat concern. 8. Ontology match_type values set to 'closest_parent' for FoodOn and FoodEx2 as no exact code exists for Indian-spiced scrambled egg preparation in either system.

q2_core_macro_completeness_and_source_in.notes: energy_kcal 150.0, protein_g 6.0, fat_g 6.67, carbohydrate_g 16.0 — all present, numeric, and consistent with ICMR-NIN egg dish ranges. nutrient_source documents ICMR-NIN as primary approved source with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as cross-reference. No forbidden sources used. Source URL points to approved ICMR-NIN and USDA FDC endpoints.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g 1.0, sodium_mg 316.67, saturated_fat_g 1.67, sugar_g 0.67 — all present as numeric values, plausible and source-supported.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index 55, gi_category 'low' (GI 55 is at the upper boundary of the low category per standard classification ≤55=low), glycemic_load_per_serving 22.0, gi_source documented with University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate basis. Estimation methodology clearly stated per Veritas GI policy. gi_category corrected from 'medium' to 'low' to match GI value of 55.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn URI provided (scrambled egg food product, closest_parent match). FoodEx2 code A00GJ (scrambled egg) used as closest defensible parent anchor with gap note. SNOMED CT null with documented gap note. No codes invented. match_type values corrected to use approved vocabulary: 'closest_parent' for FoodOn and FoodEx2, 'none' for SNOMED CT.

q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description is well above the 200-character minimum. Covers food identity, preparation method, taste/texture, cultural context, clinical relevance, and key nutrients. common_user_queries and ingredient_synonyms populated.
